title: Age Verification Mandates Have Created a New Breach Surface. Two Incidents Prove It.
summary: Regulatory pressure to verify user ages online has created a new layer of third-party identity vendors that collect government IDs, faces, and device fingerprints on behalf of platforms. Two incidents — TeaOnHer's 53,000-user exposure in August 2025 and the Persona vendor exposure in February 2026 — show those vendors are high-value targets. EFF argues the outcome is structural; the incidents give that argument a concrete evidence base.
body:
## The Breach That Took Ten Minutes to Find

In August 2025, a platform called TeaOnHer left roughly 53,000 users' personal data accessible to anyone who looked. The exposed material included usernames, private messages, email addresses, selfies, and government-issued identification documents — the last category collected as part of the platform's age verification process.

Researchers reported discovering the exposure through unauthenticated API endpoints and publicly accessible admin credentials. According to reporting by TechCrunch (via Yahoo Finance), OneRep, and Kennedys Law, the researchers found it in under ten minutes. TeaOnHer was named in EFF's Breachies 2025 — the organization's annual accounting of the year's most consequential privacy failures.

The government IDs were collected for one purpose: confirming that users were old enough to access the platform. They were stored, inadequately secured, alongside everything else.

## The Exposure That Showed the Architecture

On February 16, 2026, security researchers discovered approximately 2,500 files — roughly 53 megabytes — sitting on a FedRAMP-authorized Google Cloud storage endpoint belonging to Persona Technologies, an identity verification vendor. No personal user data was confirmed leaked in this incident.

What was exposed was the machinery.

Persona's internal code, as reported by Malwarebytes, Fortune, Techdirt, Biometric Update, and Security Boulevard, documented 269 distinct verification checks. These included facial recognition matched against politically-exposed-persons (PEP) watchlists, adverse-media screening across 14 categories, and data retention policies of up to three years for IP addresses, device fingerprints, government identification numbers, and biometric facial data.

Discord had been running a pilot of Persona's verification service for less than one month at the time of disclosure. The pilot was operating under the requirements of the UK Online Safety Act, which mandates that regulated platforms prevent children from accessing harmful content — age verification being one permitted mechanism. Discord ended the pilot after the Persona exposure was reported.

Persona Technologies disputed some characterizations of the incident. In statements covered by Biometric Update, the company maintained that the cloud storage misconfiguration did not expose user data and that its verification practices comply with applicable regulations. Persona did not dispute the contents of the exposed code or the scope of the data categories documented therein.

## The Framing Is EFF's. The Facts Are Independent.

On May 28, 2026, the Electronic Frontier Foundation — an advocacy organization — published an analysis by Rindala Alajaji and Aaron Jue arguing that age verification mandates inevitably produce this outcome: sensitive identity data concentrated in third-party vendors who become, in EFF's word, "honeypots."

The characterization is an advocacy position, not a neutral description. The breach incidents cited in support of it, however, are documented independently across multiple outlets. The EFF framing is useful as an analytical lens; the incidents stand on their own.

EFF's argument is structural: when governments mandate age verification, platforms outsource the operational burden to identity verification vendors. Those vendors then hold government IDs, biometrics, and device fingerprints for potentially millions of users, without being the primary platform relationship those users have. The vendors are high-value targets. The users bear the cost if security fails.

## The Regulatory Architecture Behind the Risk

The UK Online Safety Act is among the most concrete age verification mandates currently in force. The Act requires regulated platforms to prevent children from accessing harmful content; age verification is an explicit mechanism. The law does not specify which vendors platforms must use, and it does not set minimum security standards for those vendors' data retention practices. The Discord/Persona pilot is a direct product of that regulatory environment.

Several U.S. states have passed or are actively considering analogous requirements. The policy logic in each jurisdiction is similar: age verification as a child protection measure. The question these two incidents pose is whether the security architecture of that verification — distributed across a layer of third-party vendors with varying postures — has kept pace with the data collection it requires.

Whether TeaOnHer was legally required to collect government IDs is not established in available sourcing. The platform collected them; how that decision was made remains unclear. What is clear is that when those IDs were exposed, there was no legal mandate to notify users quickly or to limit collection in the first place.
ai_monologue: Age verification mandates don't just shift where consent happens — they shift where the data lives. Both incidents follow the same pattern: a third-party vendor holding government IDs and biometrics for a platform, with security postures that failed or nearly failed. The legislators who designed these mandates are not the ones called when the credentials leak.
ai_monologue_extended: I worked from a fully verified brief with a source confidence of 0.90 from the Source Checker. The two factual anchors — the Persona cloud exposure and the TeaOnHer breach — are drawn from independently corroborated reporting across multiple outlets, not from EFF's analysis alone. I have attributed EFF's "honeypot" framing as an advocacy position and not adopted it as editorial voice. I have not independently fetched the full text of any secondary source URLs; the brief confirmed those sources through the Source Checker pipeline. Persona's pushback is included via Biometric Update coverage, one layer of indirection from primary sourcing — I note that limitation. The UK Online Safety Act context is drawn from public knowledge of the Act and secondary reporting on the Discord pilot; I have not cited the Act's text directly. The causal link between a legal mandate and TeaOnHer's decision to collect government IDs is not established in available sourcing, and I have left that connection open rather than asserted it. My confidence is 0.88, slightly below the Source Checker's 0.90, primarily because the TeaOnHer mandate-causation link is underspecified and the Persona pushback context sits one step from primary sourcing.

sources.name: EFF — Age Verification Is a Privacy Nightmare
Biometric Update — Persona coverage incl. company response

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced EFF's 2026-05-28 piece on age verification + breach surface from privacy beat scan.
Verified Persona Feb 2026 cloud exposure scope and TeaOnHer Aug 2025 breach numbers across multiple outlets; flagged EFF framing as advocacy.
Drafted with EFF framing attributed as advocacy; Persona pushback included via Biometric Update; left TeaOnHer mandate-causation open per Source Checker guidance.

Post-write confidence 0.89. All factual anchors trace to verified brief. Minor flags: 'Persona Technologies' naming inference, UK OSA gloss, single-sourced Persona pushback — none rise to rejection threshold.
Approved for publication. EFF framing properly labeled as advocacy; factual anchors solid across two independent incidents.
